timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — The Ruined Belt: A Sign-Act

heb.paragraph: God commands Jeremiah to buy a linen belt (ʾēzôr) and wear it against his skin, then bury it at the Euphrates (Perath). When retrieved, it is "ruined and completely useless" (v.7). The ʾēzôr symbolizes intimate attachment — as a belt clings to the waist, so God bound Israel and Judah to himself (v.11). But the people, like the buried belt, have become useless through exposure to pagan influence (symbolized by the Euphrates/Babylon).

panels.ctx: The linen belt sign-act is the first of Jeremiah's symbolic actions (cf. the potter, ch. 18–19; the yoke, ch. 27–28). The choice of linen is significant — priests wore linen, and the belt's placement against the skin signifies God's intimate attachment to Israel. The Euphrates (Perath) may be the literal river or the village of Parah near Anathoth — either way, it symbolizes the corrupting influence of Babylonian culture. The belt that was meant to glorify God has rotted.

cross.note: Isaiah's sign-act of walking naked and barefoot for three years — prophetic symbolism enacted in the body.
Ezekiel's sign-acts (lying on his side, rationing food) form the most extensive collection of prophetic symbolic actions, paralleling Jeremiah's.

notes.note: The linen belt is priestly material. By wearing it unwashed, Jeremiah symbolizes Israel's unique, intimate relationship with God — like a garment against the skin.
The ruined belt: "completely useless" (lōʾ yiṣlaḥ lĕkōl). The verb ṣālaḥ ("to be useful/profitable") is negated — Israel has lost its fitness for purpose. A ruined belt serves no function.
"This wicked people, who refuse to listen to my words" — the application: the people will become like the belt, ruined and useless, because they have "followed other gods."
The wine jar proverb: every jar should be filled with wine. But God will fill the inhabitants with drunkenness — not wine of celebration but wine of judgment that produces staggering confusion.

Calvin reads the sign-act as divine condescension: "God uses visible symbols because mere words were not penetrating the people's hardened hearts." The physical demonstration is a concession to spiritual dullness.
Calvin emphasises that Israel was meant to be God's "renown and praise and honor" — like a beautiful garment displaying the wearer's glory. Instead, the garment has rotted.

The Hebrew pĕrāt may refer to the Euphrates River (requiring a 700-mile round trip) or to Parah (modern Khirbet el-Farah), a village 3 miles northeast of Anathoth. The ambiguity may be intentional — the local action symbolizes the distant exile.

The "wine jar" saying may be a drinking proverb: "Of course every jar should be filled with wine!" The prophet subverts the proverb by reinterpreting "filling" as divine judgment rather than festive abundance.

Lundbom identifies the belt sign-act as a "covenant symbol reversed." The belt represents binding closeness; its burial and ruin represent covenant dissolution. The physical object enacts what words alone cannot convey.

Brueggemann reads the sign-act as "prophetic performance art" that disrupts normal consciousness. The prophet's body becomes the message — an embodied metaphor that cannot be dismissed as mere rhetoric.

sections.header: Verses 15–27 — "Can an Ethiopian Change His Skin?"

heb.paragraph: The rhetorical question "Can a Cushite (kûšî) change his skin or a leopard its spots?" (v.23) is a proverb about the impossibility of self-transformation. The point is not racial but behavioral: just as skin color and leopard spots are innate and unchangeable, so Judah's addiction to evil has become second nature. They are "accustomed to doing evil" — the habit has become identity.

panels.ctx: The chapter's second half intensifies the warning with three images: the pride that precedes destruction (v.15–17), the royal couple going into exile (vv.18–19), and the shameful exposure of Jerusalem (vv.22, 26–27). The proverb about skin and spots (v.23) introduces a near-despairing note: can Judah change? The implied answer is devastating — not without divine intervention. This prepares for the new covenant promise of chapter 31, where God himself will transform the heart.

cross.note: The new covenant promise answers the impossible question of 13:23: what humans cannot change, God will change by writing the law on hearts.
Paul's doctrine of spiritual death and divine regeneration addresses the same impossibility — those "dead in transgressions" cannot make themselves alive.

notes.note: "Give glory to the LORD your God before he brings the darkness." The metaphor of travelers caught by darkness on mountain roads conveys the urgency — the window of light is closing.
The command to the king and queen mother to "come down from your thrones" likely addresses Jehoiachin and Nehushta (2 Kgs 24:8), deported to Babylon in 597 BC.
The Cushite/leopard proverb: habitual sin becomes irremovable character. This is not determinism but an honest assessment of how deeply ingrained Judah's unfaithfulness has become.

Calvin uses this verse to argue for the bondage of the will: "Man cannot change his own nature any more than a leopard can change its spots. Only the grace of God can transform what sin has made permanent."
The "queen mother" (gĕbîrâ) held a formal office in the Judean court, wielding significant political influence. Her dethronement alongside the king signals the total collapse of the royal house.

The reference to the kûšî ("Cushite/Ethiopian") is not pejorative but observational — dark skin was simply a recognized physical characteristic of people from Cush (modern Sudan/Ethiopia), used here as a neutral comparison point.

Lundbom identifies a progression from warning (vv.15–17) through political oracle (vv.18–19) to theological diagnosis (vv.20–27). The question of v.23 is the rhetorical climax — can the people change? The structure suggests the answer is no, not without a new divine act.

Brueggemann sees this question as the crisis point of the entire prophetic enterprise. If transformation is impossible, then prophetic exhortation is futile. The question stands unresolved until chapter 31, where God promises to do what the people cannot do for themselves.

lit.note: Chapter 13 uses two symbolic vehicles — the belt sign-act and the skin/spots proverb — to communicate the same message: Judah's corruption is irreversible without divine intervention.

themes.note: Judgment at 9 — the belt's ruin and the impossibility of self-change. Prophecy at 8 reflects the sign-act genre. Holiness at 7: the linen belt (priestly material) ruined.

hebtext.note: God commands Jeremiah to buy a linen belt (ʾēzôr) and wear it against his skin, then bury it at the Euphrates (Perath). When retrieved, it is "ruined and completely useless" (v.7). But the people, like the buried belt, have become useless through exposure to pagan influence (symbolized by the Euphrates/Babylon).

The rhetorical question "Can a Cushite (kûšî) change his skin or a leopard its spots?" (v.23) is a proverb about the impossibility of self-transformation. The point is not racial but behavioral: just as skin color and leopard spots are innate and unchangeable, so Judah's addiction to evil has become second nature. trans.title: Comparing translations in The Linen Belt

translations.text: Many days later the Lord said to me, “Go now to Perath and get the belt I told you to hide there.”
And after many days the LORD said to me, “Arise, go to the Euphrates, and take from there the loincloth that I commanded you to hide there.”
So I went to Perath and dug up the belt and took it from the place where I had hidden it, but now it was ruined and completely useless.
Then I went to the Euphrates, and dug, and I took the loincloth from the place where I had hidden it. And behold, the loincloth was spoiled; it was good for nothing.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Linen Belt within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
